summary: DHH celebrates SpaceX's successful Starship booster catch and argues that people should marvel at genuine technological progress rather than letting social media commoditize spectacular achievements. He praises Elon Musk as a 'crazy one' whose ambitious vision drives humanity forward, referencing Tesla's Cybercab reveal and robotic developments alongside the SpaceX milestone. DHH criticizes people who claim to admire rule-breaking visionaries but recoil when confronted with actual nonconformists. He argues that partisan politics should not cloud appreciation for tangible engineering breakthroughs.
key_points: Social media has commoditized spectacle, compressing historic achievements to the same level as viral cat videos
People claim to celebrate 'the crazy ones' in the abstract but resist actual nonconformists who challenge the status quo
Partisan politics — Musk shifting from 'blue team' to 'red team' — shouldn't diminish appreciation for genuine technological progress
Transformative progress depends on people with outsized, seemingly irrational ambition willing the future into existence
Most people are aggressively or passively conformist and see transgressors rather than genius
money_quotes: The spectacle has been completely commoditized. The grand gesture is gone.
It now takes effort to actually marvel when something truly spectacular occurs.
Don't be cynical. Don't let transient party politics cloud the moment.
This is exactly the kind of willing the future into existence that leaps of progress depend on.
Ambition this crazy is only likely to emerge from someone equally and sufficiently nuts.
Everyone wants to believe that they'd support 'the misfits, the rebels, the troublemakers'.
Most people are either aggressively or passively conformist. They squirm when The Crazy Ones actually attempt to change the world.
Not crazy for the sake of crazy, but crazy for the sake of progress. Demonstrable, undeniable, awe-inspiring progress.
key_insight: People romanticize visionary nonconformists in the abstract but resist them in practice, and we should override that instinct to appreciate genuine technological progress regardless of politics.
